preamble: The lenition or spirantization of a voiceless velar stop /k/ into a velar fricative /x/, a classic example of weakening in the consonant system.

examples.note: Begedkefat spirantization: post-vocalic /k/ → [x] in Biblical Hebrew. Final kaf is always [x].
High German consonant shift of final /k/ to /x/.

Partial Grimm's Law in Iranian: voiceless velar /k/ became velar fricative /x/ before another consonant. E.g., Proto-Indo-Iranian *kšap- 'night' → Avestan xšap-, Old Persian xšāyaθiya 'king'. The cluster *xš is preserved in Ossetian ӕхсин (æxsin) 'lady'.
Kurdish, like other Iranian languages, preserves the Proto-Iranian k→x shift in consonant clusters. E.g., Kurdish xwendin 'to read' shows the fricative reflex of inherited *k in cluster position.

One of the most salient diachronic features of Buryat is the spirantization of the inherited strong velar stop *k to the voiceless velar fricative /x/. This parallels the affricate spirantizations *c→s and *j→z in Buryat and represents a general weakening tendency in the consonant system. E.g., Proto-Mongolic *kele 'tongue' → Buryat xele.

Western Nuer: morpheme-final /k/ spirantizes to velar fricative [ɣ] (voiced) in intervocalic position, with possible devoicing to [x] in phrase-final position. This consonantal lenition operates as a morphophonological function in Western Nuer, affecting morpheme-final velars. The Jikany (Eastern) dialect preserves [k] in these same contexts, despite the same orthographic representation.

Kabyle spirantization: lax /k/ in post-vocalic and intervocalic positions weakens to the velar or palatal fricative [x]/[ç]. As in all Berber spirantization, geminate /kk/ resists the shift. Kabyle is classified as a spirantizing Berber language alongside other northern varieties.

Middle Atlas Tamazight (Ayt Ndhir dialect area) spirantizes lax /k/ to [x] or the palatal variant [ç]. The process is conditioned by syllable position and tense/lax phonation contrast; found throughout central and northern Moroccan Berber dialects.

sources: Waterman, J. T. (1966). A History of the German Language.
Windfuhr, G. (2009). The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
allophones_note: /k/ surfaces as [x] in post-vocalic and word-final positions in Biblical Hebrew and High German, while [k] is preserved in word-initial and pre-vocalic positions.
